title: Apple turnovers
subtitle: Puff pastry parcels with spiced apple filling
excerpt: Eight apple turnovers made with ready-rolled puff pastry and a quick cooked apple filling. Beginner-friendly because the pastry comes from the packet. The filling cooks in 10 minutes; the whole thing is assembled and in the oven in under 30 minutes.
sourceNotes: Apple turnovers are documented in British and American cookery from the eighteenth century onwards; the form appears in Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747, Project Gutenberg), as a folded pastry with fruit filling. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136), gives a comparable apple pastry. The use of ready-made puff pastry for domestic baking is documented in pre-1928 convenience cooking guides available through the British Newspaper Archive.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked from raw. Brush with egg wash and bake from frozen at the same temperature, adding 8 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Filling can be made a day ahead and refrigerated. Assemble and bake on the day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven. Conventional 210°C. High heat gives the best puff and colour.

recipeTools.notes: For re-rolling if using a block of pastry rather than ready-rolled.

glossaryTerms.definition: When folding puff pastry over a filling, the edge must be pressed firmly to prevent it opening during baking. Wet the border with a little beaten egg or water before folding, then press down with the tines of a fork or pinch firmly with your fingers. Score the top with a knife for steam vents. If the seal breaks in the oven the filling will leak and the pastry will not rise properly around it. For a triangular turnover, work from the centre of each edge outward to push out trapped air before sealing the corner.

content.text: Apple turnovers are squares of puff pastry filled with spiced apple, folded into triangles, and
before baking. Ready-rolled pastry from a supermarket packet keeps this beginner-level. Eight turnovers. Serve warm with custard or ice cream, or cold as a packed lunch.

Melt the butter in a medium saucepan over medium heat. Add the diced apple, sugar, cinnamon, and lemon juice. Cook for 8 to 10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the apple is just tender but not collapsed. Leave to cool completely before filling the pastry.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan (210°C conventional). Line a baking tray. Cut the pastry into 8 squares, roughly 12 cm across. Place a heaped tablespoon of cool filling in the centre of each square. Brush the edges with beaten egg, then fold one corner over to the opposite corner, making a triangle. Press the edges firmly with a fork to seal and make 2 small cuts in the top for steam. Brush the tops with egg wash.
Bake for 22 to 25 minutes, until deep golden and well puffed. Rest for 5 minutes before serving. Dust with icing sugar if you like.
